_purpose: County Landscape Profile for Moultrie County, IL. The smallest county in the cluster — quiet agricultural prairie centered on Sullivan, where the Kaskaskia headwaters drain flat corn country settled by Upper South families.

physical_geography.physiographic_province: Till Plains section of the Central Lowland Province. Flat Wisconsinan glacial plain. Among the smallest counties in Illinois.
physical_geography.elevation_range: 630–680 ft. Extremely flat. The Kaskaskia River headwaters provide minimal relief.
physical_geography.dominant_terrain: Level glacial till plain, almost entirely under cultivation. Sullivan (county seat) is the only significant town. The landscape is section-line roads, tiled fields, and grain elevators — the quintessential small Corn Belt county.

soil_types.description: Deep, dark, poorly drained prairie soil. Dominant soil type. World-class corn productivity.
soil_types.agricultural_impact: Moultrie County's economy was and is single-crop agriculture. The Drummer soils produced corn and soybeans at yields that ranked among the highest in the state.
soil_types.source: USDA Soil Survey of Moultrie County, Illinois. Washington, DC: Government Printing Office, 1971.

major_waterways.name: Kaskaskia River (headwaters)
major_waterways.description: The Kaskaskia begins in the northern part of the county as a modest stream. It provided water for stock and limited mill sites but was never navigable.
major_waterways.narrative_hook: The Kaskaskia River started small in Moultrie County — just a creek in the cornfields — but it ran south through four counties before reaching the Mississippi, and {ancestor_name} drank from the same water that would eventually reach the Gulf.

vegetation_eras.dominant_cover: Tallgrass prairie with scattered timber groves
vegetation_eras.description: Open prairie with timber along the Kaskaskia and its tributaries. The county was Kickapoo territory before settlement.
vegetation_eras.source: Combined History of Shelby and Moultrie Counties. Philadelphia: Brink, McDonough & Co., 1881.

vegetation_eras.dominant_cover: Prairie broken for corn; timber diminishing; small farm landscape emerging
vegetation_eras.description: Settlement from Kentucky and Virginia. Moultrie County was carved from Shelby in 1843. The county developed as purely agricultural — no significant industry, no railroad until the 1850s.
vegetation_eras.source: Combined History of Shelby and Moultrie Counties (1881).

vegetation_eras.dominant_cover: Corn and soybean monoculture; grain elevators at railroad depots
vegetation_eras.description: Moultrie County was the quietest county in the cluster — no coal mining, no significant industry, no urban center larger than Sullivan (population approximately 3,500 by 1950). The landscape was entirely agricultural: tiled fields, section-line roads, grain elevators, and small crossroads communities.
vegetation_eras.source: USDA Soil Survey of Moultrie County, Illinois (1971).

sensory_snapshots.snapshot: July in Moultrie County was corn — nothing but corn from one section line to the next, the tassels gold against dark green stalks, the air thick with pollen and humidity. The road to Sullivan was a dirt track between walls of corn taller than the wagon. The only sounds were insects, wind, and the distant clang of a blacksmith's hammer in the village. A farmwife hanging wash could see the grain elevator at the depot from her yard, the tallest structure in the county, and beyond it nothing but more corn and the flat line of the horizon. The heat was tropical — 90 degrees with humidity to match — and the only shade was in the timber along the creek where the hogs wallowed in mud black as the prairie soil.
sensory_snapshots.source: Combined History of Shelby and Moultrie Counties (1881).

sensory_snapshots.snapshot: January in Sullivan was coal smoke and silence — the town huddled around the courthouse square, the storefronts dark by 5:00 PM, and the only movement the occasional farmer's truck grinding through snow on its way to the elevator. The Depression had lowered corn prices to the point where burning corn for fuel was cheaper than buying coal in some years. The smell was wood and coal smoke, frozen earth, and the faint sweetness of stored grain from the elevator. The fields lay under snow, section-line roads drifted shut, and the isolation that the automobile had almost conquered in the 1920s returned with every blizzard.
sensory_snapshots.source: Bogue, From Prairie to Corn Belt (1963).

sensory_snapshots.snapshot: October harvest in Moultrie County was the year's climax — every hand in the county in the fields, corn shocked in rows that converged at the horizon, the air sweet with drying corn and sharp with the smell of freshly turned black soil. Wagons loaded with ear corn moved along the roads toward the elevator at Sullivan, wheels cutting ruts in dirt roads softened by autumn rain. The interurban trolley ran its electric line from Decatur through Sullivan toward Mattoon, and a farmer could hear its whistle across the flat fields a mile before it passed — the one piece of the modern world that crossed Moultrie County's agricultural landscape.
sensory_snapshots.source: Combined History of Shelby and Moultrie Counties (1881). Illinois Traction System records.

landscape_changes.description: Moultrie County was carved from Shelby County in 1843, established by settlers who wanted a closer courthouse. The county developed as purely agricultural — corn and livestock on prairie soils, settled by Upper South families from KY and TN.
landscape_changes.narrative_impact: Moultrie County was never anything but farms — if {ancestor_name} lived here, {pronoun_subject} was a farmer or served farmers, and the county's story was the corn price.
landscape_changes.source: Combined History of Shelby and Moultrie Counties (1881).

narrative_hooks: Moultrie County was the quiet heart of corn country — {ancestor_name} farmed black soil that produced more corn per acre than almost anywhere on earth, and the county seat at Sullivan was a courthouse, a store, and a depot.

The interurban trolley connected Sullivan to Decatur, and for {ancestor_name} the electric line was the difference between isolation and access — a thirty-minute ride to a city with stores, doctors, and a moving-picture theater.

In Moultrie County, the grain elevator was the tallest thing on the horizon — {ancestor_name} could see it from five miles out, and it was the only landmark in a landscape that otherwise offered nothing but corn and sky.
When corn hit ten cents a bushel during the Depression, {ancestor_name}'s neighbors in Moultrie County burned it for fuel — the corn was worth less than the coal it replaced.
source_references: Combined History of Shelby and Moultrie Counties. Philadelphia: Brink, McDonough & Co., 1881.
Bogue, Allan G. From Prairie to Corn Belt. Chicago: University of Chicago Press, 1963.
USDA Soil Survey of Moultrie County, Illinois. Washington, DC: Government Printing Office, 1971.
Illinois Traction System corporate records, Illinois State Archives.
Bateman, Newton, and Paul Selby, eds. Historical Encyclopedia of Illinois. Chicago: Munsell Publishing Co., 1912.
